summary: Gruber discusses strategies for iPhone developers to combat App Store piracy beyond Apple's universal FairPlay DRM. He responds to Marco Arment's suggestions about silent anti-piracy measures, arguing that explicit but polite messaging is more effective than silent failures. He cites Panic's experience with CandyBar, where personal appeals converted 10% of pirates to paying customers. The core problem is that FairPlay's uniformity means cracking one app cracks them all, unlike Mac apps which require individual effort. He reports that one best-selling game found roughly two-thirds of its users were running bootleg copies, undermining the argument that piracy is negligible.
key_points: Apple's uniform FairPlay DRM is a weakness: cracking one App Store app effectively cracks all of them, unlike individually-protected Mac apps
Panic's experiment showed personal, honest anti-piracy messages converted about 10% of pirates to buyers — significantly better than expected
Silent anti-piracy measures that crash the app without explanation are counterproductive, as pirates will spread word that the app is buggy rather than pirated
A best-selling iPhone game developer reported roughly two out of three users were running bootleg copies, suggesting piracy is far more widespread than commonly assumed
The jailbreak-piracy connection is inescapable: not all jailbreakers are bootleggers, but all bootleggers are jailbreakers
money_quotes: The benefit of Apple's FairPlay protection for App Store apps is that individual developers don't need to spend time working on their own protection schemes. But the problem is that the current protection scheme is universal and uniform — every licensed app from the App Store is FairPlay-protected in the same way.
So when bootleggers found a way to crack one App Store app, they found a way to crack every App Store app.
Better to make it explicit to the user what is happening. Pop up an alert that politely asks them to consider buying a legit copy.
There's a mountain of evidence that shows that this doesn't work.
Not all jailbreakers are bootleggers, but all bootleggers are jailbreakers.
The user, though a bootlegger, would be left with the impression that your app is faulty, unstable, and/or downright junky.
key_insight: Apple's uniform DRM paradoxically makes iPhone apps easier to pirate systematically than individually-protected Mac apps, and developers need their own supplementary measures — preferably honest, personal appeals rather than punitive tricks.
